title: The Eleven-Year-Old Who Listened
content: My father died when I was ten. I started caddying at Brae Burn Country Club at eleven to help support the family. I'd carry bags for these rich guys who talked about stocks like other people talked about weather. One of them gave me a tip. I bought the stock with my caddy money. It went up tenfold. I was hooked. Twelve years old and I knew what I wanted to do with my life.
extended_content: Those summers changed everything. I didn't understand half of what they said—PE ratios, earnings growth, dividend yields. But I listened. I noticed that these guys were having FUN. They talked about stocks the way I talked about baseball cards. There was a game happening, and I wanted to play. The stock tip was just luck—I didn't know anything about the company. But that tenfold return? That showed me what was possible. It wasn't about the money, really. It was about the discovery. The feeling of finding something everyone else missed. I've been chasing that feeling ever since.
when_to_tell: when someone asks how I got started or what sparked my interest
lesson: Sometimes the best education happens when you're carrying someone else's bags.
